name: Little People
description: The Little People, known as the Yunwi Tsunsdi in Cherokee, are a race of small, supernatural beings inhabiting the mountains and forests. They are generally benevolent but can be mischievous or dangerous if angered. They are often associated with healing, music, and the protection of nature. Some stories depict them as mischievous tricksters, while others portray them as wise and helpful guides. They are said to live in rock shelters, caves, and hollow logs, often near water. They are believed to possess knowledge of herbs and medicines, and are sometimes invoked for healing purposes. They are considered to be guardians of the natural world, especially the animals and plants of the forest. Their size varies in different accounts, but they are generally described as being between one and three feet tall. They are an integral part of Cherokee folklore and continue to be a significant element of their cultural heritage.
subtitle: Guardians of the Cherokee forests
appearance: The Little People are generally described as being between one and three feet tall, with brown or tan skin and long, flowing hair. They are often said to have large eyes and small, delicate features. Some accounts describe them as being dressed in clothing made from leaves and bark, while others say they are naked. Their appearance can vary, sometimes reflecting the forest around them.
habitat: They live in rock shelters, caves, and hollow logs, often near streams and waterfalls in the mountains and forests of the Cherokee territory. They are particularly associated with remote and untouched areas of the wilderness.
behavior: The Little People are generally benevolent, but they can be easily offended. They are known to be mischievous tricksters, and they may play pranks on humans who disrespect nature. However, they can also be helpful and generous to those who are kind to them. They are said to be fond of music and dancing, and they often hold gatherings in hidden glens.

keyMyths.title: The Lost Child
keyMyths.description: A child wanders into the woods and is taken in by the Little People, who care for him and teach him their ways. He eventually returns to his family, bringing with him knowledge of healing herbs.
keyMyths.source: Mooney, James. Myths of the Cherokee.

sources.author: James Mooney
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Cherokee myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Cherokee myths and legends.
